metadata.title: Capstone Unit Builder: Coherence Is the Human's Job

beats.title: Liam cold open — ClaudeComposerAsk
beats.narration_text: This is Musinique. The line that Claude's flags are questions, not decisions, and that seven coherent components don't make a coherent unit is what I need to hold onto. I have a three-week argument unit with drafted outcomes and an assessment. I want to run the coherence test myself. Can you: (1) check whether my assessment actually measures each outcome or drifts to summarizing instead of evaluating, (2) name any outcome-to-assessment gap as a question I must answer, and (3) show me what process-evidence component would close the revision-as-outcome gap the way a revision memo did?

props.command: The line that Claude's flags are questions, not decisions, and that seven coherent components don't make a coherent unit is what I need to hold onto. I have a three-week argument unit with drafted outcomes and an assessment. I want to run the coherence test myself. Can you: (1) check whether my assessment actually measures each outcome or drifts to summarizing instead of evaluating, (2) name any outcome-to-assessment gap as a question I must answer, and (3) show me what process-evidence component would close the revision-as-outcome gap the way a revision memo did?
props.segment: Capstone Unit Builder: Coherence Is the

beats.narration_text: This is Musinique. She drafted learning outcomes for a three-week argument writing unit and asked Claude to flag anything that might be hard to assess. Claude flagged outcome five: revision as process evidence — hard to assess from a final draft alone. She accepted the flag and added a revision memo component. That decision — accepting the flag, choosing the fix, adjusting the timeline — was hers. Claude drafted seven components. She checked that they cohered.
beats.new_visual_element: ClaudeComposerAsk — cold open, draft learning outcomes with assessment flags

props.command: Draft learning outcomes for a three-week argument writing unit, then flag any that might be hard to assess

beats.narration_text: The capstone unit has seven components, each draftable by Claude: learning outcomes, instructional sequence, materials and sources, feedback mechanisms, assessment design, AI-use policy, and verification checklist. Claude can draft each one. The speed is real — a full unit draft in under an hour. The risk is also real: seven individually coherent components do not automatically form a coherent unit. Coherence requires a check that Claude cannot perform.
beats.new_visual_element: Seven components — outcomes / sequence / materials / feedback / assessment / AI policy / checklist / each draftable

beats.narration_text: A release campaign is a build problem. You have a deadline, a set of deliverables — pitch email, social copy, playlist submissions, distributor forms — and a sequence that matters. The same approach that ships software on schedule ships music on schedule. The machine doesn't get tired on Friday before a Monday release.

artifactLines: The same tools the platforms use — pointed back at you.
Roll your own. Own the deck.

beats.narration_text: The coherence test is one question applied across components: does the assessment actually measure the outcomes? An outcome that says students will evaluate the strength of evidence requires an assessment that asks students to evaluate specific evidence — not to summarize an argument, not to identify claim and support. If the outcome and the assessment point at different cognitive tasks, the unit has a gap. Claude can flag it. The teacher must close it.
beats.new_visual_element: Coherence test — does assessment measure outcome? / same cognitive task / Claude flags / teacher closes

beats.narration_text: Claude's flags are questions, not decisions. 'Outcome five is hard to assess from a final draft alone' is a question: what evidence of revision would you accept? The teacher answers: a revision memo showing three changes with rationale. That answer is domain judgment — it encodes what revision means in the context of this course, this level, this pedagogical goal. Claude offers the flag. The teacher makes the instructional decision.
beats.new_visual_element: Accepting flags — flag = question not decision / teacher supplies domain judgment / what revision means in this course
beats.spark_line: Flag is a question. You supply the answer.

beats.narration_text: The verification checklist is the record: where Claude helped, where human judgment decided, what was changed from the draft and why. It is not bureaucracy. It is the evidence that a person made the instructional decisions. Claude can draft seven components. It cannot decide what learning is for. The checklist is where that boundary becomes visible.
beats.new_visual_element: Artifact view — verification checklist / where Claude drafted / where teacher decided / coherence check complete
beats.spark_line: The checklist is where the boundary shows.

beats.narration_text: Draft your first capstone component and run the coherence check. Paste this.

props.command: Draft learning outcomes for this unit: [describe it]. Then run a coherence check: (1) For each outcome, name the cognitive task it requires — recall, analysis, evaluation, synthesis. (2) For each outcome, draft an assessment prompt that targets that exact cognitive task. (3) Flag any outcome where the assessment could be satisfied without demonstrating the learning. (4) Flag any outcome that would be hard to assess from a final product alone and suggest what process evidence would show it.

beats.title: Your turn handoff — ClaudeComposerAsk
beats.narration_text: Take this prompt, run it on your own — pick any cancer type or clinical scenario you know about and ask how this mechanism applies there.

props.command: Explain how [Capstone Unit Builder: Coherence Is the Human's Job] applies to a specific cancer type or clinical case you're studying. What proteins are involved, what goes wrong at the molecular level, and what is the therapeutic or diagnostic significance?
